source: CNBC — SpaceX IPO drawdown, AI infrastructure, open source models, frontier AI
summary: We are structurally short compute; this is the dominant macro fact for AI investing
summary_extended: Baker argues that the AI industry is in a state of structural compute shortage — meaning demand for compute will persistently outpace supply over a long-term horizon, not merely as a cyclical imbalance. His reasoning is that cheaper tokens produced by open source models will stimulate dramatically more consumption of AI services, which in turn increases aggregate compute demand rather than reducing it (a Jevons paradox dynamic). This structural shortage underpins a broadly bullish view on all compute infrastructure, from chips to data centers to power. Baker frames this as the dominant macro fact for AI investing, suggesting it should be the foundational lens through which all AI infrastructure investments are evaluated. The implication is to be long compute infrastructure across the stack — GPUs, networking, data center operators, and power — as the shortage is self-reinforcing rather than self-correcting.
detail: Baker explicitly states he believes 'we are structurally short compute' as a foundational view. Cheaper tokens from open source models will drive more consumption, not less, reinforcing the compute shortage thesis. This underpins bullish views on all compute infrastructure.
confidence_evidence: Baker uses 'I do believe' framing and calls it a structural condition, not a cyclical one
quote_fragment: if you believe like I do that we are structurally short compute
